title: Feds deny Polestar authorization to sell cars in US from model year 2027
gist: The US Commerce Department has denied Polestar authorization to import cars from model year 2027 onward under rules banning connected vehicles from automakers with Chinese links. Polestar, owned by China's Zhejiang Geely Holding, will exhaust existing US inventory of Polestar 3 and 4 models but loses all future US market access, including the Polestar 5, 6, and beyond. In response, Polestar is pivoting its growth strategy to Europe, Southeast Asia, Eastern Europe, Latin America, and Canada.

themes.theme: US Regulatory Ban on Chinese-Linked Connected Vehicles

insights.claim: The US Commerce Department has denied Polestar authorization to import model year 2027 and later vehicles under a rule banning connected cars from automakers with Chinese ownership links.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Chinese-linked EV brands face a hard regulatory wall in the US market, creating a permanent bifurcation between Chinese-owned and Western-owned EV supply chains.
insights.quote: the US Commerce Department has declined to authorize imports of new Polestars from model year 2027 onward as part of a rule banning connected cars from automakers with Chinese links

insights.claim: Volvo Cars, also owned by Geely, received Commerce Department authorization to import MY27 vehicles just weeks before Polestar was denied, suggesting brand-level distinctions matter in US connected-car policy enforcement.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Regulatory outcomes vary even within the same parent holding company, implying US authorities are assessing each brand's manufacturing footprint and data architecture separately.
insights.quote: just weeks ago, Commerce authorized Volvo to import MY27 vehicles. At the time, Polestar told Ars that it was continuing to work with US authorities to meet the regulations; that work was evidently in vain.

insights.claim: Polestar's compliance efforts with US authorities ultimately failed to secure import authorization, signaling that Geely ownership is a near-insurmountable barrier for US market access regardless of manufacturing location.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Investors in Chinese-owned auto brands should not expect US regulatory relief; the bar for compliance is effectively too high even with local US manufacturing.

themes.theme: Polestar US Market Exit and Product Pipeline Loss

insights.claim: Polestar will be unable to bring the Polestar 5 sedan, Polestar 6 roadster, or any future models to the US market, representing a complete loss of its planned US product pipeline beyond current inventory.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: US revenue contributions from Polestar will trend to zero as current inventory is depleted, removing the world's largest auto market from Polestar's growth calculus.
insights.quote: we can forget about the Polestar 5 sedan, the Polestar 6 roadster, or any future models making it to these shores

insights.claim: Polestar's existing US inventory is limited to Polestar 3 and Polestar 4 SUVs, and the company will only support service for current customers going forward, effectively entering US market wind-down mode.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Polestar's US business is in terminal decline; residual value of US Polestar vehicles may deteriorate as parts and service access eventually becomes uncertain.
insights.quote: Polestar says it will continue to sell its existing stock of Polestar 3 and Polestar 4 SUVs and 'will continue to support customers, including providing access to its service network.'

themes.theme: Protectionist Auto Policy Gaining Bipartisan Support

insights.claim: US domestic auto manufacturing interests have successfully built bipartisan political support for protectionist measures against Chinese-linked automakers, making future policy reversals unlikely regardless of election outcomes.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Investors should treat US market exclusion of Chinese-linked EV brands as a durable structural condition, not a cyclical policy risk subject to reversal.
insights.quote: US domestic auto manufacturing interests have been wildly successful in raising support for protectionist measures from across the political spectrum

insights.claim: Polestar's Polestar 3 SUV is manufactured in South Carolina at the Volvo plant near Charleston, yet US-based manufacturing did not exempt it from the connected-car import ban, undermining the argument that local production mitigates regulatory risk for Chinese-owned brands.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: US manufacturing alone is insufficient to neutralize ownership-based regulatory risk; investors evaluating Chinese-owned brands with US plants should not assume local production provides regulatory insulation.
insights.quote: ironically, the Polestar 3 SUV is built in South Carolina at the Volvo plant near Charleston

insights.claim: Polestar CEO Michael Lohscheller has designated Europe as the company's largest growth engine, with the Polestar 7 planned for European manufacturing, as the company redirects investment away from the US.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Polestar's future capital allocation and model launches will be concentrated in Europe and emerging markets, not North America; European EV infrastructure and charging networks become more relevant to Polestar's investability.
insights.quote: Europe being our largest growth engine and our plan to manufacture Polestar 7 in Europe

insights.claim: Polestar is targeting Southeast Asia, Eastern Europe, Latin America, and Canada as growth markets to offset US market closure, indicating a strategy centered on markets less exposed to US-China technology decoupling.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Polestar's geographic diversification into non-US developed markets may reduce but not eliminate revenue concentration risk, as these markets are smaller and less lucrative than the US EV market.
insights.quote: we will continue to invest in markets where we have opportunities to continue to grow, like Southeast Asia, Eastern Europe, Latin America, and Canada

insights.claim: Polestar reported record sales in 2025 and Q1 2026, suggesting the company has built commercial momentum outside the US that partly cushions the blow of US market exclusion.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Record non-US sales provide a baseline that limits immediate financial catastrophe from US exclusion, but long-term valuation will hinge on whether Europe and emerging markets can absorb foregone US volume.
insights.quote: Our record sales in 2025 and the first quarter of 2026 show that we are making strong progress, with several new market launches taking place in Europe this year.

themes.theme: Geely Corporate Structure and Subsidiary Differentiation

insights.claim: Zhejiang Geely Holding owns Polestar, Volvo Cars, Lynk and Co, and Zeekr, but the US is applying differentiated regulatory outcomes across these subsidiaries, with Volvo authorized and Polestar denied for MY27 imports.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: US regulators are not treating Geely subsidiaries as a monolithic block; individual brand assessments based on manufacturing geography, data infrastructure, or brand origin create divergent US market outcomes within the same parent group.

insights.claim: Polestar manufactures primarily in China despite having South Carolina and South Korea production for US-market models, and that China-centric manufacturing base likely contributed to its failure to secure US import authorization.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Chinese EV manufacturers seeking US market access must demonstrate genuine supply chain and manufacturing separation from China, not just token US or ally-country production lines.
insights.quote: much of Polestar's manufacturing is in China
